stem: For project duration estimation, the base rate for a software project depends on which reference class is used: Reference Class A (all IT projects in the firm, n=1000): base rate = 31%. Reference Class B (similar-sized agile projects, n=50): base rate = 43%. Reference Class C (projects by this same team, n=30): base rate = 52%. Each reference class has a different level of specificity and sample size. What is the appropriate base rate to use, and how should the choice of reference class affect the uncertainty in the prediction?

ground_truth.answer: The reference class problem: different classes give rates of 31%, 43%, 52%. No single class is objectively 'correct'. Combined interval spanning all classes: [0.282, 0.686]. Weighted point estimate (specificity-weighted): 0.447.

solution_steps: Class A (all IT projects in the firm): rate=0.31, n=1000, CI=[0.282, 0.339]. Large sample but least specific.
Class B (similar-sized agile projects): rate=0.43, n=50, CI=[0.303, 0.567]. Moderate specificity and sample size.
Class C (projects by this same team): rate=0.52, n=30, CI=[0.349, 0.686]. Most specific but smallest sample (widest CI).
The reference class problem has no definitive solution — the 'correct' base rate depends on which class membership is considered most relevant.
Full uncertainty range spanning all reference classes: [0.282, 0.686].
common_failure_modes: Choosing one reference class without acknowledging the others
Using only the most specific class (Class C) despite its small sample size
Using only the broadest class (Class A) despite its low relevance
Not recognizing that the choice of reference class is itself a source of fundamental uncertainty
